1.errorText: A visitor enquires at the tourist centre about family excursions. The employee recommends a steamship cruise on the lake — the boat is over 100 years old and takes passengers out several times a day for about two hours. The mountains look fantastic from the lake. At the Country Farm, children love feeding the goats. There is also a 40-minute trek on horseback, suitable for beginners. Visitors are welcome to explore the farm on their own and there are beautiful gardens along the side of the river. The Back Road cycling trail is challenging and needs someone with a bit of experience. The bike rental place is called Ratcheson and is just by the cruise ship terminal. A repair kit is needed but there are no shops along the route.

4.scene: 商业价值观误区管理讲座

signpost.quote: I'm going to suggest that's an over-simple view. The trouble with these values is that they're theoretical concepts, removed from the reality of day-to-day business.
opts: Challenging the conventional assumption before explaining the argument

signpost.quote: On a management training course I once attended, we were put into groups and had to construct a bridge across a stream.
opts: Introducing a personal example to illustrate the first value's potential failure

signpost.quote: My point is that the task wasn't really suited to teamworking, so why make it one?
opts: Drawing a conclusion from the example before moving to the next problem

signpost.quote: It's easy to mock people who say they work hard: after all, a hamster running around in a wheel is working hard — and getting nowhere.
opts: Using an analogy to introduce the limitation of hard work as a value

signpost.quote: In business, being first with a product is more profitable than having the best product.
opts: Making a counterintuitive claim about excellence before providing evidence

items: Risk: tasks not suited to teamwork can cause
if everyone gets in each other's way
Side effect: trying to involve everyone creates

mindmap.title: Value 2 — Hard Work
items: Hard work is only valuable when properly
Best people: 'clever and lazy' — intellectual clarity; avoid
Instead of more man-hours, look for a more effective

mindmap.title: Value 3 — Creativity
items: Some campaigns remembered for creativity with no effect on sales — e.g.
Creator derives pleasure from the idea and wrongly assumes audience shares the feeling
Too many new products = too creative, but smaller

mindmap.title: Value 4 — Excellence
items: 'First' beats 'best' — pioneers averaged 29% market share vs followers' 13%
Insisting on excellence wastes energy and leads to losing
' approach is likely to be more successful

dictation.speaker: LECTURER
parts: Today I'm going to talk about four business values: collaboration, hard work, creativity and excellence. Most people would say they're all 'good things'. I'm going to suggest that's an over-simple view.
Business leaders generally try to do the right thing. But all too often the right thing backfires when they adopt values without understanding and managing the side
that arise.
On a management training course, we had to construct a
across a stream. The rule was that everyone in the team had to move at least one building block.
Our team leader simply asked everyone to move a piece a few centimetres to comply with the rule, and then let the person with an aptitude for puzzles build it alone. We avoided
and finished first.
Teamwork can also lead to inconsistency — a common cause of poor sales. A company launched a
: one director wanted to target the business market, another demanded it was aimed at consumers. The product met the needs of neither group.
A hamster running in a wheel is working hard — and getting nowhere. Hard work is valuable only when properly targeted. Otherwise it wastes

The most valuable people are those who are both clever and lazy — they possess intellectual clarity and don't rush into decisions. They come up with solutions to save the time spent by the stupid and hard-working group.
Some advertising campaigns are remembered for their creativity without having any effect on sales. This happened with the launch of a
— consumers remembered the adverts but had no idea what was being advertised.

A major study compared pioneers — first to market — with followers. Pioneers averaged 29% market share; followers achieved less than half that — only 13% — even though their product might have been better.
Insisting on excellence is time-consuming and leads to losing out on opportunities. The 'just-get-
' approach is likely to be more successful.

1.errorText: A woman calls the Kenton Festival box office for information. The festival runs from 16th to 19th May. On the opening day, people start gathering at 2 pm, and events start at 2.45. The festival will be officially opened by the mayor, followed by a performance by a children's choir. Then a community group will perform a short play about Helen Tungate, a scientist who was born in Kenton 200 years ago. The fireworks take place across the river and are best viewed from the town square. The art students' videos about the relationship between children and their grandparents are being shown in Handsworth House. Tickets for the concert can be bought online or in shops displaying the festival poster.

Carol is organising a school project about life in the town fifty years ago — the library has a new section on local history. The reference books are now in the main part of the library, but there is also a community room used for meetings and study. Susie says the children's Science Club experiments use things from the garden. The Reading Challenge requires children to read eight books during the holidays. On Friday, a local author called Tanya Streep will talk about her novel, which is based on a crime that took place overseas. The free medical check-up is only for the over-50s.

dictation.speaker: LECTURER
parts: Researching urban soundscapes used to be simple — we measured levels of sound in decibels. With data like this, acoustic engineers built up 'noise
' — maps of the sound environment.
But these maps ignore important issues such as noise from open windows or
of neighbours. And they take no account of the fact that people vary in their perceptions of noise.
Even though these maps are fairly crude, they've been useful in raising awareness that noise matters — it's a
matter that we need rules and regulations for.
Noise can cause city-dwellers to suffer interrupted sleep, and it can lead to a rise in levels of
. If schoolchildren don't have a quiet place to
, their work will suffer.
One problem with decibel measurement is that it doesn't differentiate between types of noise — a
in a town square might score quite highly, but it's not something we'd want to reduce.
Many researchers are now using
science techniques — studying people's emotional response to sound using questionnaires.
A city needs a sense of activity — the clack of high heels, the hiss of a coffee machine — but these mustn't be too intrusive. We also need to be able to
Architects and town planners receive very little
in acoustics. They currently see sound as something to be avoided, rather than an opportunity to add to the experience of urban
Noise in cities needs to be regarded as an aesthetic quality — like an art form. We need to understand the relationship between sound and
, and what we can learn from psychology and physics.
Architects already use virtual reality to show their ideas — but these programs are
. In future, such programs could use realistic sounds, meaning that soundscapes could be explored before being built.
